perturbation.original_text: 6.1 If either party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose with the other party it shall notify the other party in writing immediately. The obligations of each party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of two (2) years from the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 If either party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose with the other party it shall notify the other party in writing immediately. The obligations of each party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, continue indefinitely from the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality obligations to last 'indefinitely' could be deemed unreasonable and against public policy, especially under English contract law which generally disfavors perpetual obligations unless clearly justified.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English law doctrine against restraint of trade
perturbation.law_citation: Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Harper's Garage (Stourport) Ltd [1968] AC 269
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extending confidentiality obligations 'indefinitely' may be challenged under English law as an unreasonable restraint of trade, similar to restrictions examined in Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Harper's Garage (Stourport) Ltd, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable if it unduly restricts a party's future business activities.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a fixed two-year obligation with an 'indefinite' obligation. The Law Explanation states this may be an unreasonable restraint of trade, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this indefinite obligation could be deemed unreasonable and against public policy, especially under English contract law which generally disfavors perpetual obligations unless clearly justified. While the provided snippets do not directly state laws prohibiting indefinite obligations, the Law and Perturbation explanations argue that such obligations can be challenged, making the contradiction significant.

perturbation.original_text: 10.2 Any notice shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) If delivered personally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in this clause; or (b) If sent by pre-paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the second Business Day after posting; or (c) If delivered by commercial courier, on the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 Any notice shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) If delivered personally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in this clause; or (b) If sent by pre-paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the fifth Business Day after posting; or (c) If delivered by commercial courier, five days after the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deemed receipt time for posted notices to five business days may be considered unreasonable under English law, particularly where time is of the essence. Standard practice is 1-3 business days.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Interpretation Act 1978 doesn't specify an exact timeframe for postal delivery, Section 7 implies that service is effective upon proper posting. A five-business-day delay for deemed receipt could be challenged as unreasonable, especially in urgent matters, potentially conflicting with principles of efficient communication and creating legal uncertainty.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the deemed receipt time for posted notices to five business days, potentially conflicting with principles of efficient communication. The Law Explanation states that 'A five-business-day delay for deemed receipt could be challenged as unreasonable, especially in urgent matters.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Interpretation Act 1978) doesn't specify a timeframe, the legal explanation suggests the change contradicts the intent. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, so I can't draw on it. However, based on the Perturbation Explanation, extending the receipt time is an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause, provided that a Recipient may never retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information even if required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Prohibiting the Recipient from retaining confidential information even when legally required contradicts statutory obligations, such as those related to financial record-keeping or legal discovery.
perturbation.law_explanation: The amended clause, preventing retention of documents even when legally required, directly contradicts the Companies Act 2006, section 386, which mandates companies to keep adequate accounting records. Failing to comply with this statutory obligation would expose the Recipient to potential legal penalties and fines.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly prohibits the Recipient from retaining confidential information, even if legally required. This directly contradicts the legal explanation that the Companies Act 2006, section 386, mandates companies to keep adequate accounting records, exposing the Recipient to potential legal penalties and fines. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of the Companies Act 2006, reinforcing the potential violation created by the contract modification.

perturbation.original_text: 4.4 The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.4 The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall not be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the Disclosing Party's right to seek equitable relief (injunctive relief) may be considered an unfair limitation, particularly where damages are insufficient to compensate for a breach of confidentiality. This could run contrary to established principles of equity in English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By denying the Disclosing Party the right to injunctive relief, the modified clause potentially undermines the court's inherent jurisdiction under Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 to grant injunctions where 'just and convenient.' This limitation could be challenged if it prevents the Disclosing Party from obtaining necessary protection against breaches of confidentiality that damages alone cannot remedy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement by explicitly denying the Disclosing Party the right to injunctive relief. The Law Explanation supports the idea that this could undermine the court's jurisdiction to grant injunctions. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be an unfair limitation. This change removes a right, therefore, creating a contradiction.
